name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَيُنَزِّلُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ رِزْقًا ۚ وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَن يُنِيبُ (١٣)
```

```english
It is He who shows you His signs and sends down provision for you from the sky. Yet no one takes admonition except those who return penitently [to Allah]. (13)
```

```arabic
فَٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
So supplicate Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him, though the faithless should be averse. (14)
```

```arabic
رَفِيعُ ٱلدَّرَجَـٰتِ ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ يُلْقِى ٱلرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ لِيُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلتَّلَاقِ (١٥)
```

```english
Raiser of ranks, Lord of the Throne, He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever of His servants that He wishes, that he may warn [people] of the Day of Encounter. (15)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ هُم بَـٰرِزُونَ ۖ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَىْءٌ ۚ لِّمَنِ ٱلْمُلْكُ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ لِلَّهِ ٱلْوَٰحِدِ ٱلْقَهَّارِ (١٦)
```

```english
The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’ (16)
```

```arabic
ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ ۚ لَا ظُلْمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٧)
```

```english
‘Today every soul shall be requited for what it has earned. There will be no injustice today. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning.’ (17)
```

```arabic
وَأَنذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔازِفَةِ إِذِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ لَدَى ٱلْحَنَاجِرِ كَـٰظِمِينَ ۚ مَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ (١٨)
```

```english
Warn them of the Approaching Day, when the hearts will be at the throats, choking with suppressed agony, [and] the wrongdoers will have no sympathizer, nor any intercessor who might be heard. (18)
```

```arabic
يَعْلَمُ خَآئِنَةَ ٱلْأَعْيُنِ وَمَا تُخْفِى ٱلصُّدُورُ (١٩)
```

```english
He knows the treachery of the eyes, and what the breasts hide. (19)
```

```arabic
وَٱللَّهُ يَقْضِى بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَقْضُونَ بِشَىْءٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (٢٠)
```

```english
Allah judges with justice, while those whom they invoke besides Him do not judge by anything. Indeed it is Allah who is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (20)
```

## (Commentary)

This is an argumentation for monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and a warning following the division of people into those who return to Allah and follow His path, and those who deny the signs and argue with falsehood.

His saying, the Exalted: **هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِهِ** "It is He who shows you His signs" until the end of the verse. What is meant by the signs (الآيات) are the marks (العلائم) and proofs (الحجج) indicating His oneness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية), as evidenced by the subsequent derivation of His saying: **فَادْعُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ** "So supplicate Allah, making religion pure for Him" from it. The signs are general, encompassing the cosmic signs (الآيات الكونية) witnessed in the world by every person of sound perception, as well as the signs that occur at the hands of the messengers and the established proofs through the way of revelation (الوحي).

The sentence contains an argument: for if there were a deity whose worship was obligatory upon man, and whose worship constituted perfection and felicity for man, it would be necessary, within the perfection of governance (التدبير) and complete providence (العناية), that He guide man toward Himself. The One whose Lordship and Divinity are indicated by the cosmic signs, and whose indication is supported by the messengers and prophets through their call and the bringing of signs, is Allah, the Glorified. As for their deities whom they call upon besides Allah, there is no sign from them indicating anything. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the sole Deity, having no partner. To this argument, Ali (peace be upon him) refers in what is narrated from him: "If your Lord had a partner, his messengers would have come to you."

And His saying: **وَيُنَزِّلُ لَكُمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ رِزْقًا** "and sends down for you provision from the sky" is another argument for His oneness, the Exalted, from the perspective of provision (الرزق). For the provisioning of the servants is among the affairs of Lordship and Divinity, and provision is from Allah rather than their partners; thus, He is the Lord and Deity, not them.

The exegetes have interpreted "provision" as rain and "the sky" as the direction of height. It is not improbable that "provision" refers to the very things by which one is sustained, and their "sending down from the sky" refers to their emergence from the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — into the manifest world (الشهادة), according to what is conveyed by His saying: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ** **There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure.** (15:21).

And His saying: **وَمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ إِلَّا مَنْ يُنِيبُ** "and no one takes admonition except him who returns penitently" is a parenthetical statement explaining that the attainment of remembrance (التذكر) through these proofs is only the characteristic of one of the two aforementioned groups, namely the penitent (المنيبون) who return to their Lord, excluding the faithless (الكافرين) who argue. For disbelief (الكفر) and obstinate denial (الجحود) nullify the capacity for remembrance through proof and the following of truth.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَادْعُوا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْكَافِرُونَ** **So supplicate Allah, making religion pure for Him, though the faithless should be averse.** (40:14). It is more appropriate to the context that the address be general to the believers and others, derived from the preceding argument, except that it does not include the disbelievers mentioned at the end of the verse, who are the deniers that argue with falsehood.

It is as if it were said: Since the signs indicate His oneness, the Exalted, and He is the Provider, then those other than the disbelievers who denied and argued must supplicate Allah, making religion pure for Him. As for the disbelievers who loathe monotheism, there is no hope for them; no sign benefits them and no proof convinces them. So worship Him with sincerity (الإخلاص) and let the disbelievers loathe that.

His saying, the Exalted: **رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ ذُو الْعَرْشِ يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَى مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ** "Exalter of ranks, Lord of the Throne, He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants" etc. These are three attributes of Him, the Exalted, each being a predicate after a predicate for the pronoun in His saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِهِ** "It is He who shows you His signs." The verse and what follows it are directed toward warning (الإنذار).

Various interpretations have been offered for His saying: **رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ** "Exalter of ranks." It has been said to mean: the One who raises the ranks of the prophets and saints in Paradise. It has also been said: the One who raised the seven heavens from which the angels ascend to His Throne. It has been said: the One whose Throne's points of ascent are high. And it has been said: it is a metaphor for the loftiness of His status and authority (السلطان).

What reflection (التدبر) yields is that the verse and what follows it describe His kingdom, the Exalted, over His creation: that He has a Throne (العرش) in which the reins of the affairs of creation are gathered, and from which the command (الأمر) descends, being exalted by high ranks (درجات رفيعة) which are the levels of His creation. Perhaps these are the heavens which He described in His speech as the dwellings of His angels and that His command descends between them, and they are what veil His Throne from the people.

Furthermore, He has a day, which is the Day of Encounter (يوم التلاق), in which the veil between Him and the people is lifted by the removal of the covering from their insights, the folding of the heavens with His right hand, and the manifestation of His Throne to them. It will then be disclosed to them that He is the King over all things; there is no kingdom except His kingdom, and He shall judge between them.

Thus, what is meant by "ranks" (الدرجات) are the levels from which one ascends to His Throne. His saying: **رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ ذُو الْعَرْشِ** "Exalter of ranks, Lord of the Throne" becomes a metaphorical allusion to the transcendence of the Throne of His kingdom above the level of creation, and its absence and concealment from them before the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) by high ranks and distant stages.

And His saying: **يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَى مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ** "He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants" is an allusion to the matter of the message (الرسالة) whose function is warning. The qualification of the Spirit (الروح) by His saying: **مِنْ أَمْرِهِ** "of His command" is proof that what is meant by it is the Spirit He mentioned in His saying: **قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي** **Say, ‘The Spirit is of my Lord’s command’** (17:85), which is that which accompanies the angels of revelation, as indicated by His saying: **يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَى مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ أَنْ أَنْذِرُوا** **He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants: ‘Warn...’** (16:2).

Thus, what is meant by "casting the Spirit" upon whomever He wishes is sending it down with the angels of revelation upon him. What is meant by His saying: **مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ** "whomever He wishes of His servants" are the messengers whom Allah has chosen for His message. Regarding the meaning of the Spirit cast upon the Prophet, there are other opinions that are not worth considering.

And His saying: **لِيُنْذِرَ يَوْمَ التَّلَاقِ** "that he may warn of the Day of Encounter." This is the Day of Resurrection; it is so named because of the meeting of the creatures therein, or the meeting of the Creator and the created, or the meeting of the inhabitants of heaven and earth, or the meeting of the oppressor and the oppressed, or the meeting of a man and his deeds. Each of these views has its proponents.

The second view may be supported by the repeated mention in His speech, the Exalted, of the "encounter" (اللقاء), such as His saying: **بِلِقَاءِ رَبِّهِمْ لَكَافِرُونَ** **are indeed disbelievers in the encounter with their Lord** (30:8), and His saying: **إِنَّهُمْ مُلَاقُو رَبِّهِمْ** **they will indeed encounter their Lord** (11:29), and His saying: **يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسَانُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَى رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَاقِيهِ** **O man! You are laboring toward your Lord laboriously, and you will encounter Him.** (84:6). The meaning of the encounter (اللقاء) is the severing of distracting causes (الأسباب), the manifestation that Allah is the Manifest Truth, and their emergence before Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ هُمْ بَارِزُونَ لَا يَخْفَى عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ** "The day when they will emerge: nothing about them will be hidden from Allah" etc., is an explanation of the Day of Encounter. The meaning of their "emergence" (بروزهم) before Allah is the manifestation of that to them and the removal of the illusory causes that used to pull them toward themselves, veiling them from their Lord and making them heedless of the encompassment of His kingdom, His exclusivity in judgment, and His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity.

Thus, His saying: **يَوْمَ هُمْ بَارِزُونَ** "The day when they will emerge" is an allusion to the removal of every veiling cause. His saying: **لَا يَخْفَى عَلَى اللَّهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ** "nothing about them will be hidden from Allah" is an explanation of the meaning of their emergence before Allah and a clarification; for their hearts and deeds are before the eye of Allah, and their outward and inward, what they remembered and what they forgot, are exposed and not concealed.

And His saying: **لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** **‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’** (40:16). This is a question and an answer from His side, the Glorified, through which the reality of the Day is explained: it is the manifestation of His kingdom and authority, the Exalted, over the creation absolutely.

In His being described as "the One, the All-paramount" (الواحد القهار), there is a justification for the restriction of sovereignty to Him. For since He has vanquished (قهر) everything, He owns it and dominates it by stripping it of independence, and since He is One, the sovereignty belongs to Him alone.

His saying, the Exalted: **الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ لَا ظُلْمَ الْيَوْمَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسَابِ** **Today every soul shall be recompensed for what it has earned. There will be no injustice today. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning.** (40:17). The preposition "with" (بـ) in "for what it has earned" (بما كسبت) denotes connection, and the intent is to explain the characteristic of the Day: that every soul is recompensed with the very thing it earned, so its recompense is its deed. The Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** **O you who have disbelieved! Do not make any excuses today. You are only being recompensed for what you used to do.** (66:7).

And His saying: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسَابِ** "Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning" is a justification for the negation of injustice in His saying: **لَا ظُلْمَ الْيَوْمَ** "There will be no injustice today." That is, He, the Exalted, is swift in reckoning; the reckoning of one soul does not distract Him from the reckoning of another such that He might err and recompense a soul with other than its due, thereby treating it unjustly.

This justification looks toward the negation of injustice arising from error. As for injustice arising from intent and knowledge, its absence is a foregone conclusion, because since the recompense is the deed itself, injustice is inconceivable.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْآزِفَةِ إِذِ الْقُلُوبُ لَدَى الْحَنَاجِرِ كَاظِمِينَ** "And warn them of the Approaching Day, when the hearts will be at the throats, choking with grief" until the end of the verse. "The Approaching" (الآزفة) is one of the descriptions of the Resurrection, meaning that which is near and imminent. The Exalted said: **إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُ بَعِيدًا وَنَرَاهُ قَرِيبًا** **Indeed they see it to be far off, and We see it to be near.** (70:6-7).

And His saying: **إِذِ الْقُلُوبُ لَدَى الْحَنَاجِرِ كَاظِمِينَ** "when the hearts will be at the throats, choking with grief." The "throats" (الحناجر) is the plural of throat (حنجرة), which is the top of the windpipe from the outside. The hearts being at the throats is a metaphor for extreme fear, as if they shift from their place and reach the throats due to the intensity of terror. "Choking with grief" (كاظمين) is from "choking" (الكظم), which is the intensity of sorrow.

And His saying: **مَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ** **The wrongdoers will have no sympathetic friend, nor any intercessor who might be heard.** (40:18). The "sympathetic friend" (الحميم) is the close relative; that is, they have no relative who would stand to help them out of the zeal of kinship. The Exalted said: **فَلَا أَنْسَابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ** **there will be no kinship between them on that day** (23:101). Nor is there an intercessor (شفيع) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — whose intercession is obeyed.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَعْلَمُ خَائِنَةَ الْأَعْيُنِ وَمَا تُخْفِي الصُّدُورُ** **He knows the treachery of the eyes, and what the breasts conceal.** (40:19). It has been said: "treachery" (خائنة) is a verbal noun like "treason" (الخيانة), similar to "lie" (كاذبة) and "vain talk" (لاغية) meaning lying and vanity. What is meant by the treachery of the eyes is not every sin of theirs, but rather the sins that do not appear to others, such as a stolen glance, as evidenced by its mention alongside what the breasts conceal.

It has also been said: "the treachery of the eyes" (خائنة الأعين) is a case of an attribute being added to the described object, which necessitates that "knowledge" (العلم) here means "cognition" (المعرفة), and the meaning would be: He knows the treacherous eyes. The first view is the correct one.

And His saying: **وَمَا تُخْفِي الصُّدُورُ** "and what the breasts conceal" refers to what the soul keeps secret and hides of the forms of disbelief, hypocrisy, and the dispositions of sins.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللَّهُ يَقْضِي بِالْحَقِّ وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لَا يَقْضُونَ بِشَيْءٍ** **Allah judges with justice, while those they invoke besides Him do not judge by anything.** (40:20). This is another argument for His uniqueness, the Exalted, in Divinity, which He established after mentioning the exclusivity of sovereignty to Him on the Day of Resurrection and His knowledge of the treachery of the eyes and what the breasts conceal, as a preparation and introduction.

Its summary is that it is a necessary requirement in Divinity that the Deity judge among His servants and between them. Allah, the Glorified, is the One who judges between the creation and among them on the Day of Resurrection, while those they invoke besides Him do not judge by anything because they are owned servants who possess nothing.

Among His judgment, the Exalted, is His governance of the particulars of His servants' affairs through creation after creation, for that is the manifestation of judgment (القضاء) and decree (الحكم). The Exalted said: **إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** **All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is.** (36:82), and He said: **إِذَا قَضَى أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** **When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is.** (3:47). Since no one besides Him, the Exalted, has a share in creation, no one has a share in judgment.

Also among His judgment, the Exalted, is the legislation of religion and His approval of it as a path for Himself. The Exalted said: **وَقَضَى رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ** **Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship anyone except Him** (17:23).

And His saying: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ** **Indeed Allah is the All-hearing, the All-seeing.** (40:20). That is, He possesses the reality of knowledge of things heard and seen by His essence (لذاته), while no one else possesses any of that except what Allah has granted and permitted, not by their own essence.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَى مَنْ يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ** "He casts the Spirit of His command upon whomever He wishes of His servants," he said: "The Holy Spirit (روح القدس) — and it is specific to the Messenger of Allah and the Imams, صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of transmission from Hafs ibn Ghiyath, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Day of Encounter (يوم التلاق) is the day when the inhabitants of heaven and the inhabitants of earth meet."

I say: It was also narrated by al-Qummi in his *Tafsir* as an unattributed and broken narration.

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Faddal, from al-Rida, from his fathers, from Ali (peace be upon him), in a narration where he said: "And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ** 'To whom does the sovereignty belong today?' Then the spirits of His prophets, His messengers, and His proofs speak, saying: **لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** 'To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!' Then Allah, majestic is His majesty, says: **الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ** 'Today every soul shall be recompensed for what it has earned' the verse."

In *Nahj al-Balagha*: "And He, the Glorified, shall return after the annihilation of the world alone, with nothing alongside Him, just as He was before its beginning; so shall He be after its annihilation, without time (وقت), period (زمان), moment (حين), or place (مكان). At that point, terms and times shall cease to exist, and years and hours shall vanish. There shall be nothing but Allah, the One, the All-paramount, to whom is the return of all affairs. Without power from them was the beginning of their creation, and without resistance from them was their annihilation. Had they the power to resist, their existence would have endured."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with its chain of transmission from Thuwayr ibn Abi Fakhtah, from Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him), who said: "He was asked about the two blasts [of the trumpet]: how much is between them? He said: 'Whatever He wills.'"

Then he (peace be upon him) mentioned the manner of the blast and the death of the inhabitants of the earth and heaven — until he said — "They remain in that state for as long as Allah wills. Then He commands the heaven and it heaves, and He commands the mountains and they move — and that is His saying: **يَوْمَ تَمُورُ السَّماءُ مَوْراً وَتَسِيرُ الْجِبالُ سَيْراً** **The day when the sky will heave with a [terrible] heaving, and the mountains will move with a [terrible] moving.** (52:9-10). That is, it is spread out and **تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ** **the earth is turned into another earth** (14:48) — meaning an earth upon which sins have not been earned — manifest, with no mountains or vegetation upon it, just as He spread it the first time. And He returns His Throne upon the water as it was the first time — independent in His greatness and power."

He said: "At that point, the Compeller (الجبار), majestic is His majesty, calls out with a loud voice from His side, which is heard in the regions of the heavens and the earths: **لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ** 'To whom does the sovereignty belong today?' But no one answers Him. At that point, the Compeller, the Mighty and Majestic, says, answering Himself: **لِلَّهِ الْوَاحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** 'To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!'" the narration.

I say: Reflection on the last three narrations guides to the fact that what is annihilated of the creation is the independence of their existence, their relations, and the links of causality between them, as conveyed by the Quranic verses. The spirits do not die, and there is no time between the two blasts, so do not be heedless. In the narrations are subtle indications that appear to the reflective mind, and among them are those whose outward meaning differs from what preceded.

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), in a narration where he said: "O Abu Ahmad, there is no believer who commits a sin except that it distresses him and he regrets it. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: 'Regret suffices as repentance,' and he said: 'Whoever is pleased by his good deed and distressed by his evil deed is a believer.' So if he does not regret a sin he commits, he is not a believer, and intercession is not mandatory for him, and he is a wrongdoer (ظالماً). And Allah the Exalted says: **مَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ** **The wrongdoers will have no sympathetic friend, nor any intercessor who might be heard.** (40:18)."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of transmission to Abd al-Rahman ibn Salamah al-Hariri, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يَعْلَمُ خَائِنَةَ الْأَعْيُنِ** 'He knows the treachery of the eyes.' He said: 'Have you not seen a man looking at something as if he is not looking? That is the treachery of the eyes.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Abu Dawud, al-Nasa'i, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Sa'd, who said: "On the day of the conquest of Mecca, the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) granted amnesty to the people except for four men and two women, and he said: 'Kill them even if you find them clinging to the curtains of the Kaaba.' Among them was Abdullah ibn Sa'd ibn Abi Sarh, who hid with Uthman ibn Affan."

"When the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) called the people to the pledge of allegiance, he [Uthman] brought him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, accept the pledge of Abdullah.' He looked at him three times, each time refusing to accept his pledge. Then he accepted his pledge, then turned to his companions and said: 'Was there not among you a right-minded man who would stand up to this one when he saw me withholding my hand from his pledge and kill him?' They said: 'We did not know, O Messenger of Allah, what was in your soul. Why did you not signal to us with your eye?' He said: 'It is not befitting for a prophet to have treachery of the eyes (خائنة الأعين).'"
